title: Polymer clay abstract swirl bowl
subtitle: A shallow bowl draped inside a mould with a swirled multicolour surface
excerpt: A shallow serving bowl shape formed by draping a marbled polymer clay slab inside a small oven-safe bowl mould. The swirled marbled surface is made by twisting and folding two or three colours together before rolling the slab. No kiln or wheel required.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay marbled slab drape mould technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Twisting two or more colours of polymer clay together and rolling to create a swirled, unpatterned surface that reveals different colours when cut.
A form over which a clay slab is draped to give it a curved shape during drying or setting.
Working polymer clay with the hands until it is soft, warm, and uniformly pliable before shaping.

content.text: produces a different swirl each time. The key is to stop blending before the colours merge into a muddy uniform tone. The
can be any oven-safe bowl or dish: a small ceramic ramekin or a silicone mould works well.

items.name: Small oven-safe bowl for mould (100-130 mm diameter)
Baking paper or cling film to line the mould

content.text: complete when the clay is soft throughout with no crumbly edges. Roll each colour into a rough log. Twist the two or three logs together once or twice, then fold in half and twist again. Repeat four or five times. Do not over-blend: stop when you can still see distinct colour swirls rather than a blended mix. Roll the combined clay into a slab 3-4 mm thick.

Line the inside of the bowl mould with baking paper or cling film to prevent sticking. Cut the slab into a rough circle slightly larger than the bowl opening. Lay the slab over the inside of the bowl and press it gently to conform to the curve, smoothing from the centre outward. Trim the edge level with the rim of the mould using the craft knife. The slab should sit inside the mould without visible folds.

Bake in the mould at the clay manufacturer's temperature (usually 130 degrees C) for 30 minutes. Allow to cool completely inside the mould before removing. The bowl retains the curved shape after baking. Sand the rim with fine sandpaper to remove any cut marks. Buff with a soft cloth for a satin finish. Polymer clay bowls should not be used for food; use as a decorative dish or jewellery tray.
Make a set of three small pinch dishes (60 mm diameter) with complementary colour combinations.
Add a thin sheet of metallic gold clay beneath the marbled layer so it shows through when the slab is thinned.
Use a rectangular mould for a tray shape rather than a bowl.

rows.problem: Colours blended to a uniform muddy tone
rows.cause: Too many twists and folds during marbling
rows.fix: For the next piece, stop after 4-5 twist-and-fold cycles. The colours should still be visibly distinct.

rows.problem: Slab cracked when pressed into the mould
rows.cause: Clay was insufficiently conditioned, or the slab was rolled too thin
rows.fix: Condition more thoroughly. Keep slab thickness above 3 mm for bowls of this size.

rows.cause: Clay was removed from the mould before it cooled fully
rows.fix: Leave the bowl in the mould until completely cool at room temperature. Polymer clay is soft while hot.
